title: Photon position eigenstates in configuration space
abstract: The expressions of the eigenfunctions of the Hawton photon position operator in the configuration space are derived for several classes of wave function, including the Riemann-Silberstein and Landau-Peierls cases. Although these eigenfunctions have a simple form in momentum space, the explicit characterization of their representations in the configuration space is rather more involved. We provide closed expressions of these eigenfunctions in terms of linear combinations of the complete elliptic integrals $K(κ)$ and $E(κ)$ with modulus $κ$ depending on trigonometric functions of the polar angle. We show that they diverge not only at the value $\mathbf q$ of the position eigenvalue, but also on a plane containing $\mathbf q$ and that they decay as inverse powers of the distance from $\mathbf q$.
summary: This paper provides the first explicit, closed-form mathematical descriptions of photon position eigenfunctions in configuration space. It reveals that these wave functions are not localized at a single point but exhibit a singular plane of divergence. This work advances the theoretical understanding of how light is represented in coordinate space.
main_problem: The paper addresses the long-standing problem of photon localization by seeking explicit, closed-form expressions for the eigenfunctions of the Hawton photon position operator in configuration space.
main_result: The authors derive closed-form expressions for the Landau-Peierls and Riemann-Silberstein position eigenfunctions using complete elliptic integrals, revealing that these eigenfunctions possess a singular plane of divergence and decay as inverse powers of distance.
method: The study employs 3D Euclidean momentum space analysis, Fourier transforms, and the use of the Hertz superpotential, utilizing mathematical tools such as Gauss hypergeometric functions and regularization of divergent integrals.
model_or_system: The system is a first-quantized theory of photons (massless particles with helicity sigma = +/- 1) described by a family of Hawton position operators and wave functions related to the Riemann-Silberstein and Landau-Peierls electromagnetic field representations.
key_observables: Momentum, Hamiltonian, Helicity, and Photon Position Eigenfunctions.
important_parameters: Helicity (sigma), wave function type parameter (alpha), scalar product parameter (beta), and the radial distance (r) and polar angle (theta).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to proper orthochronous Poincaré transformations and assumes a dynamical superselection rule for helicity; certain integrals require regularization for specific ranges of beta.
figures_summary: Figure 1 plots radial and angular components of the eigenfunctions to highlight the singular line at theta = pi/2; Figure 2 shows contour plots of the RS eigenfunction components in the (x, z) plane.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the photon position operator and wave functions in momentum space, proceeds to derive the configuration-space eigenfunctions using Fourier transforms and elliptic integrals, analyzes the resulting singularities and asymptotic behavior, and concludes with a Taylor expansion for the time-dependent Hertz superpotential.
why_interesting: This paper provides fundamental mathematical clarity on the spatial structure of photon wave functions, which is crucial for anyone working on the foundations of quantum optics and the localization of light.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Plots of the functions 𝑃𝜌(𝜃), 𝑃𝜓(𝜃), and 𝑃𝑧(𝜃) for the LP and RS photon position eigenfunctions centered at the origin (cf. eqs. (48)- (49)). The vertical dashed line in these plots is the singular line 𝜃= 𝜋/2.

FIG. 2. From top to bottom: contour plots in the (𝑥, 𝑧) plane of the 𝜌, 𝜓, and 𝑧components of the RS photon position eigenfunction with positive helicity centered at the origin, in units of (ℏ𝑐)1/2. (The white area indicates the region where the absolute value of these functions is greater than 1.5.)
